pattern: Whether an element qualifies depends only on how many elements outrank it, and that rank is the same question for every equal value, so sorting once and asking a 'how many are strictly greater' question per element (via a binary search) replaces a pairwise comparison with a lookup.
hints: An element qualifies purely based on how many other elements are strictly larger than it -- its own position in the array never matters.
Sort the array once; for any value, the count of elements strictly greater than it is just the array length minus how many elements are less than or equal to it, which a binary search finds directly.

Special-case k = 0 up front (every element trivially has at least zero greater elements, so the whole array qualifies) and otherwise binary-search each original element's value against the sorted copy.

edge_inputs.description: statement example 2, all equal so nothing qualifies
k = 0 means every element automatically qualifies
single element array with the only valid k (0)
k equal to n - 1 only the single minimum element can qualify
duplicate maximum values: none of them have anyone greater

stress.description: large random array with a random threshold k
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
    return [nums, k]
